<!-- Topic 3: Comparing All Three Types -->
<!-- Total slides: 4 -->

# Comparing All Three Types

---

## Three array types — which one? {.smaller}

- `int[]` — low-level C array
- `std::array<T,N>` — fixed-size with STL features
- `vector<T>` — dynamic with safety

::: notes
Total slides: 4
:::

<!-- Slide 1 -->

---

## Side-by-side comparison

| | `int[]` | `array<T,N>` | `vector<T>` |
|---|---|---|---|
| Size | Fixed, compile-time | Fixed, compile-time | Dynamic, runtime |
| Storage | Stack | Stack | Heap |
| STL features | None | Yes | Yes |
| Pointer decay | Yes | No | No |
| `.size()` | No | Yes | Yes |

<!-- Slide 2 -->

---

## When to use each

| Situation | Choose |
|---|---|
| Size changes at runtime | `vector<T>` |
| Size fixed, need STL or clean function passing | `array<T,N>` |
| C library interop, no overhead, legacy code | `int[]` |

- Default to `vector` for general work
- Reach for `std::array` when size is compile-time fixed and you need `.size()` or no-decay passing
- Use `int[]` only when interfacing with C APIs or when the environment forbids heap allocation

<!-- Slide 3 -->

---

## Summary

- All three are contiguous, same-type sequences — they differ in size flexibility, STL support, and passing behavior
- Default to `vector`; use `std::array` for fixed-size collections; use `int[]` only when the context demands it

<!-- Slide 4 -->
